attributes.stem:
A 60-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a six-month history of urinary incontinence. She reports leaking urine when coughing or laughing. She denies any sensation of urinary urgency or difficulty getting to the toilet. She drinks 2L of fruit flavoured water per day, two cups of tea and two cups of coffee, and opens her bowels every two days which is consistent for her. She has a medical history of three normal vaginal deliveries. She has no other medical conditions and takes no regular medications.

She has tried pelvic floor exercises for the past two months with a physiotherapist but still has persisting issues and does not want surgery.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her BMI is 30 kg/m2. Pelvic examination reveals no evidence of prolapse, mild atrophy and the bladder is not palpable. Urinalysis is negative for nitrites and leukocyte esterase.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of stress urinary incontinence, characterised by involuntary urine leakage with increased intra-abdominal pressure during activities like coughing and laughing. The increased BMI and previous vaginal deliveries would increase her risk of having stress incontinence from weakness of the urethral sphincter. The absence of urgency and no issues in getting to the toilet helps differentiate this from urge incontinence, mixed incontinence or functional incontinence.

Oestrogen cream is an appropriate treatment for stress incontinence in postmenopausal women to improve urethral mucosal integrity and vascularity in cases of atrophic vaginitis. It should be used alongside pelvic floor exercises and lifestyle modifications.

Limiting fluid intake at nighttime may help reduce nocturia but would not address the underlying mechanism of stress incontinence. Whilst she drinks four caffeinated drinks per day, it does not affect the cause of her incontinence. Additionally, adequate hydration throughout the day is important for overall health.

Oxybutynin is an anticholinergic medication used for overactive bladder and urge incontinence. It would not be appropriate for stress incontinence and may cause side effects such as dry mouth and constipation.

Increasing dietary fibre may help with constipation but would not directly address stress incontinence. While good bowel habits are important for pelvic floor health, this would not be the most appropriate primary intervention, nor does she have any issue with constipation. Second daily bowel opening is normal for her and there has been no recent change of this.

Botulinum injection is used in the treatment of overactive bladder and detrusor overactivity but has no role in stress urinary incontinence. In fact, it may worsen symptoms by weakening the urethral sphincter.

The management of stress incontinence should include a combination of approaches including pelvic floor exercises, weight loss (particularly relevant given this patient's BMI of 30 kg/m2), and appropriate pharmacological therapy such as topical oestrogen in postmenopausal women. Mid-urethral sling could also be considered for difficult cases.

A vaginal pessary is another non-surgical option that could be considered for this patient. Pessaries provide mechanical support to the urethra and bladder neck, which can reduce stress incontinence symptoms. They are particularly useful for women who have concurrent pelvic organ prolapse or those who are not suitable for surgery. However, they require regular follow-up for cleaning and checking for complications such as erosion or infection.
learningPoints: Pelvic floor exercises are first-line treatment for stress urinary incontinence, with better evidence than medications.
Weight loss should be encouraged in patients with stress incontinence and elevated BMI.
Topical vaginal oestrogen can improve urethral mucosal integrity in postmenopausal women with stress incontinence.
references: RACGP aged care clinical guide (Silver Book): Urinary incontinence. In: The Royal Australian College of General Practitioners. 2019. Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/clinical-resources/clinical-guidelines/key-racgp-guidelines/view-all-racgp-guidelines/silver-book/part-a/urinary-incontinence

Brown J, Bradley C, Subak L et al. The sensitivity and specificity of a simple test to distinguish between urge and stress urinary incontinence. Ann Intern Med. 2006; 144(10):715-723. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16702587/

Australian Commission on Safety and Quality in Health Care. Treatment options for stress urinary incontinence (SUI): Transvaginal mesh – information for consumers [Internet]. Sydney: ACSQHC; 2020 [cited 2025 May 27]. Available from: https://www.safetyandquality.gov.au/sites/default/files/2020-11/treatment_options_for_stress_urinary_incontinence_sui_-_transvaginal_tv_mesh_-_information_for_consumers_patient_resource.pdf

Hey-Smith E, Berghmans L, Hendriks H, de Bie R, van Waalwijk van Doorn E. Pelvic floor muscle training for urinary incontinence in women. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2001;(1):CD001407. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11279716/

McDonald J, Homewood D, West C, Fong E, Elmer S. Managing female stress urinary incontinence in a post mesh era: What to do and when to refer. Aust J Gen Pract. 2024;53(5):283–7. Available from: https://www1.racgp.org.au/ajgp/2024/may/managing-female-stress-urinary-incontinence-in-a-p
